I disagree that the end-to-end principle necessarily applies here. The irc library is intended to not be a simple broker of network message, but also an interpreter of IRC messages, providing to the Python programmer a sensible, intuitive interface. The first feature of the library indicated in the docstring of client.py is that the module is an "Abstraction of the IRC protocol." In the case of Python 3, providing only bytes objects is poor form, and contrary to what I see from other protocol handlers (such as HTTP handlers). In this aim, it was intentional that the irc library add decoding of incoming messages.

You bring up a very good point that the RFC does not specify an encoding (and in fact indicates that clients may send messages in any encoding, leaving it up to the client to interpret those bytes as appropriate). However, it's also been my observation that the vast majority of clients also now use UTF-8 by default (or at least configure for UTF-8 encoding for the most popular servers). And for many consumers of the IRC library (including pmxbot, which I maintain), accepting Unicode for outgoing messages and returning Unicode for incoming messages has some strong benefits.

That said, I agree that simply raising a UnicodeDecodeError when non-UTF-8 input is received is incorrect, in light of the RFC and especially in light of the expectations of irker. Also, simply doing a decode with `error='replace'` as the 3.2.2 release does is also undesirable.

Therefore, my inclination is to leave the UTF-8 decoding on by default, but allow hooks for consuming libraries (such as irker) to override the decoding behavior of input (and possibly also the encoding behavior of output).

Would configurable decoding behavior be sufficient for your use case? If this configuration were available per-process, would that be sufficient, or should the encoding be configurable per-server as well? I'm tempted to take the easy route and only make it configurable per-process (globally), and then wait to see if a use-case arises that demands per-connection behavior.

Image Quilting might be not so trivial to implement in the library. It sounds like comparisons with the whole dataset of images are required, which likely would require an additional variable in each augmenter object (responsible for image quilting) storing that dataset. That would then cause problems with background augmentation, as running that in C processes will require C copies of the dataset in RAM (and possibly another one in the main process). Not a problem for small datasets, but for the ones with 10gb and more in size that can quickly cause issues.

The `--use-local` option searches for packages that are locally installed to the same Python environment as your `pipreqs` install. In this case, that's your Anaconda install. Your `pip` points to your macOS system Python, so if you install things using `pip install` and then try to collect those packages via `pipreqs --use-local`, your environments will differ and will likely produce incorrect results.

To solve this issue, make sure your `pip` and `pipreqs` both live in the same Python environment. The easiest approach here is to use a [virtualenv](https://virtualenv.pypa.io/en/stable/) or a [conda environment](https://conda.io/docs/user-guide/tasks/manage-environments.html).

This doesn't appear to be a `pipreqs` bug, so I'm going to close this issue. If you find that this behavior persists even when working within a single Python environment, we can reopen it.

Ah.  I see that you have fallen into the async close() trap.   I was wondering how long it was going to take for someone to explode their head on this... ;-).

Let me preface this by saying that I do NOT know the correct answer to the problem, but here are some of the reasons why I defined it as an async method.

1.  Consistency.  All of the other methods concerning I/O are async methods so for the purpose of uniformity in the API, close was also defined as async.  Likewise, only an async-context manager is provided. 

2.  I/O Buffering.  Curio allows stream-like objects to be wrapped with async.  Under the covers, streams might be using some kind of I/O buffering.   When you close a stream, the contents of the underlying buffer might not yet be written.  Thus, there is an implicit flush() operation that takes place.  In performing the flush(), it's possible you'll take an exception on non-blocking I/O.   So, to handle that, you'd have coordinate back with the scheduler.  This means that it has to be async.   This issue is related to this http://bugs.python.org/issue25476

Concerning context-managers, I didn't provide sockets with a normal context manager (__enter__, __exit__) primarily because of the same issue, but also to prevent possible mistakes.  For example, using a synchronous context manager in a context where an asynchronous one should have been used.

Again, I don't know the "answer" on this, but this is a weird corner of the whole API.   Part of a bigger discussion where async is allowed and not allowed perhaps.

Take my +1 for "Seperate task manager app" approach which would not
penalize anyone who never needs such a functionality. Better crash handling
may seem fine at first sight. However things that should be done on a crash
is completely application dependent.

Even if curio crash handling is improved "what will it do?" Restart??
Definetely not a good idea.. Your use case declares clean up, restart,
continuation, termination conditions.

I always write Python code as if it is C++ with complete control
on the resources it manages. No general cleverness exists to
deal with messy coding.. If you can't trust your code then
write "try:" on the very first line of your coroutine.
And spend some time developping a good task hieararcy
with bullet proof task management strategy specific to your
application. Nobody can give it to you..

@fcurella 

> [...] it looks like 'Pinyin' would be the de-facto 'standard' romanization. The only issue is that those names still contain accented letters, which are outside of the ascii range. Would it be appropriate to use that romanization and simply strip the accents?

I would say yes. This is pretty much what Chinese people will do when confronted with an ascii-only form.  Technically you can write the tones (i.e. the accents) by appending numbers (e.g. mā  má  mǎ  -> ma1 ma2 ma3).

Also there is also the use of ü, which isn't actually a tonal mark, but indicates a change in vowel sound.  Stripping the umlaut in this case is approximately the same as stripping them from the German ü.

Separating into two issues.

**Good news: i found the reason for the problem described above. (not yet fixed in code!)**

Like assumed, the introduction of the auxiliary was the reason! Because i wanted my function-arguments to mimick the ones of OneHot and co. i introduced the auxvarname-argument. After debugging the newly introduced unit-tests & rereading part of the docs, it seems that a variable-object is completely defined by it's name at the time of creation!
This is the source of the problems described above!

Easy case:
- ExactlyN() uses AtLeastN() & AtMostN() internally, but called both with the same auxvarname! -> BUG!
- **fixed in code**

Not that easy case:
- ExactlyN()/AtLeastN()/AtMostN() might be called multiple times during the generation of some complex expression
- The auxvarnames introduced musn't collide! -> If they do -> BUG!
- **NOT fixed in code**
- *Question: How to handle this? Is there already some kind of functionality in the lib?*
 - I suppose the library should handle this automatically (some auxvarname-prefix/suffix), but the following shows a customized **workaround** for the sudoku example **which works**:
   - idea: use a "global" generator introducing a suffix for each auxvarname

```python
""" The following is the pyeda sudoku example using ExactlyN() instead of OneHot()
"""

from pyeda.boolalg.expr import And, OneHot, NHot, ExactlyN, AtMostN, AtLeastN, expr2dimacscnf
from pyeda.boolalg.bfarray import exprvars
import itertools

gen = itertools.count()

DIGITS = "123456789"

X = exprvars('x', (1, 10), (1, 10), (1, 10))

V = And(*[
        And(*[
            ExactlyN(*[X[r,c,v] for v in range(1, 10)], k=1, auxvarname='AUX_'+str(next(gen)))
                     for c in range(1, 10)]
        )
        for r in range(1, 10)]
    )

R = And(*[
        And(*[
            ExactlyN(*[X[r,c,v] for c in range(1, 10)], k=1, auxvarname='AUX_'+str(next(gen)))
                     for v in range(1, 10)]
        )
        for r in range(1, 10)]
    )

C = And(*[
        And(*[
            ExactlyN(*[X[r,c,v] for r in range(1, 10)], k=1, auxvarname='AUX_'+str(next(gen)))
                     for v in range(1, 10)]
        )
        for c in range(1, 10)]
    )

B = And(*[
        And(*[
            ExactlyN(*[
                X[3*br+r,3*bc+c,v]
                    for r in range(1, 4)
                    for c in range(1, 4)], k=1, auxvarname='AUX_'+str(next(gen))
            )
            for v in range(1, 10)]
        )
        for br in range(3) for bc in range(3)]
    )

S = And(V, R, C, B)

# This step does absorption
S = S.to_cnf()

def parse_grid(grid):
    chars = [c for c in grid if c in DIGITS or c in "0."]
    assert len(chars) == 9 ** 2
    return And(*[ X[i // 9 + 1, i % 9 + 1, int(c)]
                  for i, c in enumerate(chars) if c in DIGITS ])

grid = ( ".73|...|8.."
         "..4|13.|.5."
         ".85|..6|31."
         "---+---+---"
         "5..|.9.|.3."
         "..8|.1.|5.."
         ".1.|.6.|..7"
         "---+---+---"
         ".51|6..|28."
         ".4.|.52|9.."
         "..2|...|64." )

def get_val(point, r, c):
    for v in range(1, 10):
        if point[X[r,c,v]]:
            return DIGITS[v-1]
    return "X"

def display(point):
    chars = list()
    for r in range(1, 10):
        for c in range(1, 10):
            if c in (4, 7):
                chars.append("|")
            chars.append(get_val(point, r, c))
        if r != 9:
            chars.append("\n")
            if r in (3, 6):
                chars.append("---+---+---\n")
    print("".join(chars))

def solve(grid):
    with parse_grid(grid):
        return S.satisfy_one()

bla = solve(grid)
display(bla)

```

In general, the best way to set it up locally for testing is to use a [virtualenv](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/virtualenv). Then you do the following (assuming you're on Linux or Mac, I'd have to double check Windows ;)):
```bash
# Create the virtual environment
/path/to/zap-cli$ virtualenv venv
# activate the virtual environment to start using it
/path/to/zap-cli$ source venv/bin/activate
# install the dev version of zap-cli
/path/to/zap-cli (venv)$ pip install -e .[dev]
```

Once you do that, the `zap-cli` command should be in the `venv/bin` directory, and while the virtual environment is activated should be in your path. Let me know if that works, and thanks for putting this PR together! :)

Does the form-based authentication you have configured work for your site?

A simple way to test if it works is to:
1. Open ZAP and have your browser proxied through it (with a new session in ZAP, and not being logged in to the site in your browser)
2. Import your context with the saved authentication method
3. Ensure you have the user credentials for at least one account configured in the context
4. Enable "Forced User Mode" (there's an option in the top menu)
5. Refresh a page on your site and see if you are now logged in

If it's configured properly, then ZAP should log you in to the configured user credentials using the form-based authentication you set up. If it doesn't, something is wrong with the authentication setup.

If it's not working, I'd suggest reading over https://github.com/zaproxy/zap-core-help/wiki/HelpStartConceptsAuthentication and also check if form-based authentication is sufficient for the site you're testing. For example, if the login form has CSRF protection it won't work, and you may need to write a script for script-based authentication.

Hey @wlblount,

Sorry for the late reply. 

```WeighTarget``` will set the target weights at each point in time in the target weights DataFrame. If the first entry in the target weights DataFrame is on 12/31/2000, ***bt*** will not rebalance until then. You will have to modify your target weights DataFrame to include the dates you are interested in. I'm guessing you are using something like this for the conversion:

```python
tw = data.resample('A').last()
``` 
If this is the case, A means "Year End Frequency". If you want to rebalance right from the start, you could use AS (year start frequency) instead (see http://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/timeseries.html#offset-aliases for other options). Alternatively, you could keep the year end method but manually insert the first (initial) weights as of the first day in your dataset. 

Hope this helps,
Phil

    """

    :return:

    memo = memo or set()

    # if we have collected the subclasses for this already, return

Your test example contains a signature placeholder. The `xmldsig.sign()` method disregards that placeholder and attaches a signature immediately following it. However, the `xmldsig.verify()` method finds the placeholder, as it comes before the real signature, parses it, and fails to validate it.

To fix this, excise the signature placeholder from your document before signing it.

Well, it is quite a trivial utility as you can get the same with:

```python
for index, obj in enumerate(iterable):
    last = index == len(iterable) - 1
```

But that doesn't work with generators:

```python
for index, obj in enumerate(generator):
    last = index == len(generator) - 1   # TypeError
```

(And also, there's boilerplate.)

So I've been using:

```python
for line, last in lookahead(iterable):
    if last:
        ...
```

Unfortunately, use cases are specific, I don't think there are generic examples to share.

There are already few "lookahead" libraries out there:

- [lookahead](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/lookahead)
- [lookaheadtools](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/lookaheadtools)

These seem to provide ability to peek the next/previous values, in a bit more advanced way.

I, personally, haven't needed that, but rather:

- [iterstuff](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/iterstuff)

It provides `.atend`, `.atstart` and `.peek` properties. It doesn't allow to peek backwards, nor multiple values forward, but probably fills the "common need". Something like this is what I'd be happy to see in boltons.

(Btw, I haven't used any of these 3 libraries, just googled them up.)

> I added methods min and max to class Table which returns element with min/max value in specified field. I need it in my project and I want to share. What do you think about it?

Using the Table class for this feels wrong to me. Altough -- as you mentioned -- it currently can't be implemented using the Query class as it only selects elements based on the current value. This is also true for other queries that would act on the list of all matching queries (e.g. select every other item, select the n-th item etc.).

As far as I see, the solution @eugene-eeo proposed be the simplest one -- if you want to have the semantics of the logical AND operation. So would be equivalent:

```python
db.search((where('field1') == '1') & (where('field2').max()))
# and
max(db.search(where('field1') == '1'), key=lambda el: el["field2"])
```

On the other hand, you cannot use the proposal to express disjunction (logical OR):

```python
db.search((where('field1') == '1') | (where('field2').max()))
```

Interestingly, to express that in MySQL, you'd have to use the `GROUP BY` clause for min/max. It gets even messier (subqueries etc.) if you want to use other aggregate functions. And IMO we shouldn't replicate that complexity in TinyDB.

So yeah, I think min/max queries are beyond what's possible with TinyDB.

@msiemens it is hard what you ask as I do not really know `TinyDB`, so do not know how much my ideas would break code/upset users.

Anyway, here are my thoughts in the order of least noticeable to most radical:

----
I'd get rid of `__del__` as it is problematic and is a fallback only for the case when calling `close()` is forgotten.

----
I'd get rid of `TinyDB.close()` and provide a context manager only interface, thus forcing users to use it properly with `with` statements: `__enter__` could return a value that provides the `TinyDB` API, but the returned object's class is not public.

something like
```python
@contextlib.contextmanager
def TinyDB(*args, **kwargs):
    # create and manage private storage here
    storage = get_storage(*args, **kwargs)
    db = _TinyDB(storage)
    try:
        yield db
    finally:
        storage.close()

class _TinyDB(object):  # ~ as current TinyDB, but not exported
    ...
```
The context manager interface and `close` on `Table` is not needed.

`TinyDB` and `Table` has a strange relation: `TinyDB` is_a `Table` in a duck typing sense.
I assume this is because there are users who are OK with only one table, or even it is the most common use case.
So I'd reverse the relationship - provide a table centered API from the start and make the database object an implicit internal detail of tables.
- `tinydb_open(..., name='_default')`  -> context manager for the database, returning a `Table`, to be used like this:

    ```python
    with tinydb.open(..., name='_default') as table:
        main_program(table)
    ```
- new `Table` methods that are currently on `TinyDB` for advanced users with multiple tables:
  - `Table.siblings()` -> `set(Table names)`
  - `Table.sibling(name)` -> `Table`
  - `Table.purge_siblings()`

In particular, how to handle nested peaks or a peak in one file that overlaps multiple in another file. `bedtools multiinter` seems to make reasonable choices about these cases which is why I prefer it. I didn't really feel like writing my own theme mapping the wal colours to specific emacs attributes, so the base16 package was perfect.

Effectively, I generate the following `base16-wal-theme.el` on wal reload (right now I literally have a script echoing it to a file, I'd like to make something somewhat more elegant at some point):

```
(require 'base16-theme)

;; colours generated dynamically by wal
(defun set-wal-colors () (setq base16-wal-colors
			       '(:base00 "#352224"
					 :base01 "#8F8F92"
					 :base02 "#A39A9B"
					 :base03 "#ACAFB3"
					 :base04 "#C6B1AF"
					 :base05 "#B6BCC1"
					 :base06 "#BBC2C7"
					 :base07 "#C8CACD"
					 :base08 "#857a7b"
					 :base09 "#8F8F92"
					 :base0A "#A39A9B"
					 :base0B "#ACAFB3"
					 :base0C "#C6B1AF"
					 :base0D "#B6BCC1"
					 :base0E "#BBC2C7"
					 :base0F "#C8CACD")))

(defvar base16-wal-colors nil "All colors for base16-wal are defined here.")
(set-wal-colors)

;; Define the theme
(deftheme base16-wal)

;; Add all the faces to the theme
(base16-theme-define 'base16-wal base16-wal-colors)

;; Mark the theme as provided
(provide-theme 'base16-wal)

(provide 'base16-wal)
```

And have in my `.emacs`:

```
(defun refresh-theme ()
  (progn
    (load-theme 'base16-wal t)
(refresh-theme)
```

Now evaling `(refresh-theme)` reloads the theme. I run emacs as a daemon, so I get my `wal-set` script to call `emacsclient --eval '(refresh-theme)'` for me, but you could probably get the same effect using [file-notify](https://www.gnu.org/software/emacs/manual/html_node/elisp/File-Notifications.html).

`MissingRequiredArgument` is raised when a command is invoked without a required argument:
https://github.com/Rapptz/discord.py/blob/1863a1c6636f53592519320a173ec9573c090c0b/discord/ext/commands/errors.py#L68-L71  This means that the user of the bot triggers the command and doesn't give required input.
In this case, you're invoking the command with `!tod`, but not passing the required `user` argument, with something like `!tod Harmon758#0004`.
To handle this, you can either set a default value for the user kwarg or you can catch `MissingRequiredArgument` in `on_command_error`:
https://github.com/Rapptz/discord.py/blob/1863a1c6636f53592519320a173ec9573c090c0b/discord/ext/commands/bot.py#L286-L295  Or as that documentation states, in a specific error handler for that command:
https://github.com/Rapptz/discord.py/blob/1863a1c6636f53592519320a173ec9573c090c0b/discord/ext/commands/core.py#L376-L381
Since you seem to only want the user who triggered the command, then instead of requiring an input for the user, you should just use `ctx.message.author`:
https://github.com/Rapptz/discord.py/blob/1863a1c6636f53592519320a173ec9573c090c0b/discord/ext/commands/context.py#L37-L38 https://github.com/Rapptz/discord.py/blob/1863a1c6636f53592519320a173ec9573c090c0b/discord/message.py#L51-L53
For future questions like this, you should join either the [official discord.py server](https://discord.gg/r3sSKJJ) or the [Discord API server](https://discord.gg/discord-api) for help, as the README recommends.
Also, you should use [code blocks](https://help.github.com/articles/basic-writing-and-formatting-syntax/#quoting-code) instead of inline code formatting when you have newlines in your code.

Got it -- thanks. The issue here is that compressed messages (at least for v0 and v1 format) are actually returned as single message "batches" in the MessageSet format. The batch offset is equal to the last message in the compressed batch. When seeking to an offset that is in a compressed batch we first register the outer batch message offset, which is the offset of the last message inside the batch. When we take uncompressed messages from inside the batch, the individual messages have smaller offsets than the outer batch offset. The `_append` logic in fetcher.py assumes that each message has a larger offset than the messages that came before it. We should fix this and document this difference in offset behavior for compressed messages.

I don't believe not using join is a mistake .   in the real code "MyThread" monitors for an external event (arrival of a particular UDP packet) and dispatches a child thread for execution.  The child thread then lives its own life.  The next trigger for a new thread may and does often arrive before the previous child thread(s) have finished running.  Putting a join there would totally break this logic.  I believe  join is used when threads have some sort of dependency and need to be serialized, but it's not the case here.

The plan to use `extras_require={'tls': 'twisted[tls]'}` was very smart, but unfortunately still doesn't work upstream.  There are open bug reports still for this feature:

- https://github.com/pypa/pip/issues/3516
- https://github.com/pypa/pip/pull/3878

For this reason, I've change the `install_requires` to specify `twisted[tls] >= 16.4.0` directly.  I know you wanted to keep the SSL-related dependencies separate if possible, but it doesn't seem like there is an easy answer here.  Suggestions?

For the documentation, I've added instructions on how to build it in the `README.markdown` file.  I've also changed the target build directory (the HTML output) to be inside the `doc/` directory, and added `doc/stompest-doc` to the `.gitignore` list. It felt more natural to me to have the documentation build output appear where `$ make html` is run.  I also deleted the `make` executable script, since it seemed tied to a specific environment configuration that required virtualenvwrapper, and other environment variables.  In further support of the documentation, I added an `extras_require={'doc': 'sphinx'}` so that sphinx can be installed this way.  This is explained in the README.  

Everything else, such as the changes in dates, and CHANGES.txt and version numbers and so on have been done as you described.

No, I think you already found out that `word2phrase` is not `doc2vec`.

The output of `word2phrase` is a text file that then you can use as input to `word2vec`.

The output of `word2vec` will have a modified version of the input text and it will merge "phrases" (ngrams) like "Los Angeles" to "Los_Angeles".

Although I don't know the specifics on what SQL Server does, I can understand and explain why this behaviour is by design and quite natural: the protocol works on a request-response model, where the client sends the request to the server and then waits for a reply. Multiple SQL statements can be submitted in one batch in order to minimise the number of network round-trips, but it is important that the client still know the status/result of each statement in a batch. Thus, the server's main loop is: read a statement; execute it; write the result. If the server can't write, it (implicitly) waits until it can.

> This is where it would be appropriate to throw on buffer overflow, or to throw on timeout (while waiting for client to consume from output buffer).

This is a network buffer, whose fullness is highly dependent on network conditions --- thus your observation that the number of inserts executed varies somewhat randomly --- and also why "throw on" doesn't make much sense in this context: I suppose it could time out and close the connection, but that would have a huge negative effect on reliability, and be essentially unusable over slower links or interactive applications/those which take a longer time to process resultsets.

Regardless, the server expects you to read (and do error checking) the replies it sends. Hence,

> Microsoft recommends that you always consume all result sets from SQL Server regardless of the size of the batch that you are executing. If you do not flush this data and there are successful result sets to be returned ahead of the error in the result set batch, the client might not discover the server errors.

Try to clear the pending results first, and if that does not fix this issue, we can investigate further.

If you want to override the entire collection, you'll need to either delete and re-created it, or use `overwrite=True` like the error suggests. If you'll notice the error message reads:
`Collection already exists. To overwrite, use 'overwrite=True'`.

So... your code should look something like this:

```python
import pystore

pystore.set_path('C:\sample_database')
store = pystore.store('store')

# create the collection
collection = store.collection('collection2')

# delete the collection
store.delete_collection('collection2')

# re-create the collection
collection2 = store.collection('collection2')
```

Or (shorter):

```python
import pystore

pystore.set_path('C:\sample_database')
store = pystore.store('store')

# create the collection
collection = store.collection('collection2')

# overwrite the collection
collection2 = store.collection('collection2', overwrite=True)
```

Hi Jonas,

I'm working with @Bensk1 on the same project.

We experienced quite often within the recent weeks that 5 seconds were simply not enough. The request timed out. Using 10 seconds as default value worked for us.

Setting `constants.CONNECTION_TIMEOUT = 10` has no effect: During the prior `from TwitterAPI import constants` TwitterAPI's `__init__.py` is loaded which loads the TwitterAPI module which loads all constants.
So when setting a custom timeout value it is already too late as the TwitterAPI module will use the default values loaded earlier.
I've voluntarily kept this very simple, as I don't know if the project owner wish to add a `wikipedia.set_timeout()` method and give flexibility to the duration of the timeout.

If you want to help, I need the log of the http request traffic created when you arm/disarm your camera.

To do this:
1. Use Chrome.
2. Right-click anywhere on the page and choose "Inspect" from the context menu. This will open the developer console.
3. Click on the Network tab.
4. Check the "Preserve log" checkbox.
5. Click the "Clear" icon (it looks like a circle with a slash through it).
6. Arm your camera from the browser.
7. Copy the http requests which show up in the developer console and paste them to me here. Please include the "Request Payload" from the "Headers" tab for each http request.
It will look something like this:
```
{"from":"336-XXXXXXX_web","to":"XXXXXXXXXXXXX","action":"set","resource":"modes","transId":"web!baf3e5e8.a1353!XXXXXXXXXXXXXX","publishResponse":true,"properties":{"active":"mode1"}}
```

Also, if Arlo is treating your Arlo Q Plus camera as a basestation, you might be able to call:
```
arlo.Arm(camera)
```
instead of:
```
arlo.Arm(basestation)
```

PyRestTest offers a couple features to assist with CI integration.  One, upon completion, the process returns a status code equal to the number of test failures.  Two, you can also set the logging level on command line execution (i.e. --log=ERROR) so it only shows log lines in case of error. 

Unfortunately, there is no support for custom output formats yet; perhaps you could assist with defining what you'd expect output to look like? Yes. I kind of "fixed" this by doing the following for chromecast audio. 

build a youtube search algorithm. You would place your song name inside the query string var below. Once song is located we use youtube_dl library to extract the audio link. We dont download it... Simply extract the link, and then send that link over to the chromecast

import urllib #for youtube parsing
import youtube_dl #use for youtube download
import re #for youtube parsing

def youtubeSearch(SongSearchL):
    try:
        returnlist = []
        f= {}
        f["search_query"] = SongSearchL
        query_string = urllib.urlencode(f)
        final_string = "https://www.youtube.com/results?" + query_string
        html_content = urllib.urlopen(final_string)
        search_results = re.findall(r'href=\"\/watch\?v=(.{11})', html_content.read().decode("utf8"))
        urlfunc = ("http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=" + search_results[0])
        opts = {'format':'bestaudio/best'} #set weird options
        ydl = youtube_dl.YoutubeDL(opts) #make ydl object
        with ydl: 
            result = ydl.extract_info(urlfunc,download=False) # We just want to extract the info
        url = result.get('url')
        trackt = result.get('title')
        returnlist.append(url)
        returnlist.append(trackt)
        return returnlist
    except:
        return "BIG ERROR"

Hi @21stio.  Thanks for your interest in inferface!

There isn't currently a public API for checking if an object/class implements an interface.  The closest thing is `Interface.verify(type)`, which is what gets called at class creation time when you declare that you implement an interface, but that function is fairly expensive when it fails, because it spends a lot of effort building nice error messages.

I think it makes sense to add a function that just gives back a boolean indicating whether an object/type implements an interface (though I confess I haven't actually needed such a function yet in using interface, which is why it currently doesn't exist).  What would you expect the behavior of such a function to be?  If we added a function named something like `check_implements` (actual name TBD), I can think of a few potential axes of variation.

1. Would `check_implements(obj, IFace)` expect `obj` to be a type or an instance?  Would we want separate methods for objects and types (analogous to the built-in `isinstance`/`issubclass` functions)?
2. Would `check_implements` just verify that the object it receives was declared as implementing `IFace`, or would it dynamically verify that the interface is actually implemented?  The former would likely be much faster, but I could imagine use-cases for both behaviors.

When calling `pytest` in an env, it's important you're actually calling it from the env... for instance, consider:

    $ which pytest
    //anaconda/bin/pytest
    $ source activate py35
    (py35) $ which pytest

Notice it's pointing to the same `pytest` in both envs (not always the case, but if you have not explicitly conda installed pytest in the env itself, it uses the root version). You should use:

@ilevkivskyi : I agree that's a reasonable interface and implementation of a new, simpler `make_class()` (although modifying the passed-in namespace seems a little icky, but we can discuss that later).

But I think it still doesn't provide all of the possible functionality, such as metaclasses, exposed for example in `types.new_class()`.

In any event, I'm going to merge my PR #29 and drop the existing `make_class()` and references to it in the PEP. I'll have the PEP contain a simple example, such as in my [comment above]( https://github.com/ericvsmith/dataclasses/issues/27#issue-255784324).

I'm going to leave this issue open, as a reminder to address it later. Whether that's in dataclasses.py itself, or in a dataclasses_tools on PyPI, or just through docs, we can decide later.

I think the PEP is improved by focusing on:

> `dataclass` is just a function, usually but not always used as a decorator, that will add some methods to your class. You provide the class, using a class statement or whatever mechanism you want, subject to a few simple rules, and it does the rest.

If the fields values are unorderable, you do get a TypeError:

```
@dataclass
class C:
    i: int
    c: complex

print(C(0, 1j) < C(0, -2j))

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "i.py", line 44, in <module>
    print(C(0, 1j) < C(0, -2j))
  File "<string>", line 3, in __lt__
TypeError: '<' not supported between instances of 'complex' and 'complex'
```

Which is slightly worse that what you get with `order=False`:

```
@dataclass(order=False)
class C:
    i: int
    c: complex

print(C(0, 1j) < C(0, -2j))

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "i.py", line 44, in <module>
    print(C(0, 1j) < C(0, -2j))
TypeError: '<' not supported between instances of 'C' and 'C'
```
I don't have a feel for how often instances will be ordered. But it's true that I don't often try to order `attrs` instances. I guess `attrs` doesn't have this issue because they group our `eq` and `order` together in their `cmp`, and you definitely want `__eq__` and `__ne__` by default.

My gut feeling is that we should leave `order=True` as the default if comparing unorderable fields raises TypeError.

If no one chimes in here with an opinion, I'll raise this on python-dev (or feel free to do that yourself).

The 4 cases above don't cover my last proposal, so let me describe it here in the same manner as the above:

### 5. We leave ``consume`` as backward-compatible function and introduce ``create_consumer`` without callbacks

This is effectively proposal 4, but we leave ``consume``. This solves the issue of backward incompatibility issue.

```Python
c = queue.create_consumer()
# then...
yield from c.get()
```

Pros:
* Always impossible to ignore errors - they will be thrown as exceptions into your coroutine.
* We can deprecate ``consume`` if we think that is appropriate.
* It uses the same object for cancelation ``c.close()``
* Compared to proposal 3 we don't create the object ourselves so never have an issue on calling ``c.close()`` before we actually started a consumer
* We can provide very clean and descriptive documentation on this method. In all other proposals we will need to describe it inside 1 big description of ``consume``. Even now it is very confusing.

Cons:
* Users of the ``callback`` style can not make their own ``QueuingConsumer`` by subclassing one.
* Users can't do something ``unusual``

In the end, I decided to customize the `revision.revert` method as follows:

```
def revert(revision, delete=True, keep_parents=False):
    # Group the models by the database of the serialized model.
    versions_by_db = defaultdict(list)
    for version in revision.version_set.iterator():
        versions_by_db[version.db].append(version)
    # For each db, perform a separate atomic revert.
    for version_db, versions in versions_by_db.items():
        with transaction.atomic(using=version_db):
            # Optionally delete objects no longer in the current revision.
            if delete:
                # Get a set of all objects in this revision.
                old_revision = set()
                for version in versions:
                    model = version._model
                    try:
                        # Load the model instance from the same DB as it was saved under.
                        old_revision.add(model._default_manager.using(version.db).get(pk=version.object_id))
                    except model.DoesNotExist:
                        pass
                # Calculate the set of all objects that are in the revision now.
                current_revision = chain.from_iterable(
                    _follow_relations_recursive(obj)
                    for obj in old_revision
                )

                items = [item for item in current_revision if item not in old_revision]
                if not keep_parents:
                    items = [item for item in items if not item._meta.parents]

                # Delete objects that are no longer in the current revision.
                collector = Collector(using=version_db)
                collector.collect(items)
                collector.delete()
            # Attempt to revert all revisions.
            _safe_revert(versions)
```

Basically, I added a `keep_parents` parameter. If `False` then ignore parent items from deletion. Hope this helps.

@ojarva Aside from the `BaseException` and `Exception` base exception classes, there are no built-in exception names that end with `Exception`, here's an excerpt from the [exception hierarchy](https://docs.python.org/3/library/exceptions.html#exception-hierarchy):

```
BaseException
 +-- SystemExit
 +-- KeyboardInterrupt
 +-- GeneratorExit
 +-- Exception
      +-- StopIteration
      +-- StopAsyncIteration
      +-- ArithmeticError
      |    +-- FloatingPointError
      |    +-- OverflowError
      |    +-- ZeroDivisionError
      +-- AssertionError
      +-- AttributeError
      +-- BufferError
      +-- EOFError
      +-- ImportError
      +-- LookupError
      |    +-- IndexError
      |    +-- KeyError
      .
      .
      .
      +-- ValueError
      |    +-- UnicodeError
      |         +-- UnicodeDecodeError
      |         +-- UnicodeEncodeError
      |         +-- UnicodeTranslateError
      +-- Warning
           +-- DeprecationWarning
           +-- PendingDeprecationWarning
           +-- RuntimeWarning
           +-- SyntaxWarning
           +-- UserWarning
           +-- FutureWarning
           +-- ImportWarning
           +-- UnicodeWarning
           +-- BytesWarning
           +-- ResourceWarning
```

This is essentially because [there are two types of Python exceptions: errors and warnings](http://stackoverflow.com/a/28971626/6461688). Warnings don't necessarily need to be caught, but errors should be caught and handled.

[The official Python tutorial calls their user-defined exceptions `*Error`](https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/errors.html#user-defined-exceptions).

I agree that these exceptions should be caught and handled, but according to PEP-8, that means it should end with `Error`.

Here's the direct quote from [PEP-8](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/#exception-names):

> ## Exception Names ##
> 
> Because exceptions should be classes, the class naming convention applies here. However, you should use the suffix "Error" on your exception names (if the exception actually is an error).

You mean, how to generate sound output with python-rtmidi on OS X?

python-midi only sends MIDI messages and they do not make sounds by themselves, they only tell a MIDI receiver what to play etc. So you need a synthesizer to turn the MIDI commands into sound. AFAIK, OS X does not have a builtin MIDI synthesizer like Windows does (nor does Linux, btw).  So you need to install one. An example would be a soundfont player like fluidsynth resp. Qsynth (GUI for fluidsynth). See this page for more information:

https://github.com/wbsoft/frescobaldi/wiki/MIDI-playback-on-Mac-OS-X

After you start Qsynth, you should be able to see its MIDi input port with `MidiIn.get_ports()`. Then open the port and just start sending MIDI messages to it. See the example scripts in the `examples/basic` directory.

Flask Security sets `confirmed_at` [here](https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-security/blob/fc8d27fe41cdc2d1028e1e89d6315e8ba9396e77/flask_security/views.py#L233-L237) inside the `confirm_email` view. The intent of this workflow is to ensure that the registered user has access to the email they're signing up with. The process goes like:

1. User signs up with email address.
2. Flask Security sends a unique confirmation link to that email address.
3. User follows the confirmation link, and Flask Security sets the `confirmed_at` datetime attribute on the user object.

When the confirmable option is enabled, Flask Security's login function prevents the user from logging in before they've completed step 3. If `confirmed_at` is set outside of this process, there's no guarantee that the user was actually able to receive email at their address, so the feature is effectively circumvented.

If your goal is to prevent users who have not confirmed their email addresses from logging in, the standard behavior should work for you without modification. Removing your event handler should get your application to that state.

We needed this change to all us to use `;` or `{` in database passwords. Others might have similar issues with such characters in database name, user name, DSN etc.

There are two things about this change that I think are worth noting:

- Dropping the `os.path.isabs(driver)` check. This was added by #27 to support iODBC which presumably didn't like the driver path being wrapped in the `{}` escaping. Prior to #27, the escaping of the driver value was done all the time, regardless of whether or not it was necessary. With this change, the driver value is only escaped if it contains a semicolon, and most sensible file system paths do not contain semicolons. If a path to the driver did contain such a value, presumably iODBC would be unable to handle this anyway if it doesn't like the escaping.

- Restructuring the code to use a `dict` for `cstr_parts` instead of a list will result in the KeyValuePairs of the final connection string coming out in a different order. As order of KeyValuePairs is only relevant when there are repeated keys (see [[MS-ODBCSTR]](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/ee208909%28v=sql.105%29.aspx)) this should not cause any problems, but there could be bad drivers out there that do rely on a specific ordering.

So in summary, these changes shouldn't cause any problems, but there is a small risk of oddness with badly behaved drivers and setups that I do not have available to test against. It would be possible to make a more minimal change to avoid any chance of these potential issues, but I think the resulting code is cleaner and more maintainable this way.

Look at astronautlevel's answer. Apparently the zipfile module looks for a specific "end of central directory structure" at the end of the file stream. Ah, right, sorry. The structure that you are using is unusual, the Socket.IO handlers appear to be methods of your `Server_SocketIO` class but they are really not.

I recommend you move those out of the class so that they don't look like methods. In any case, to be able to access all your other objects you can save the server instance at the module level, and then you would use it instead of the `self` that I used in my previous example.

```python
my_server = None

class Server_SocketIO:
	def __init__(self, clientManager):
                global my_server
                my_server = self
		self.clientManager = clientManager

@sio.on('SetDeviceParameters')
async def on_SetDeviceParameters(sid, *args):
        receivedData = args[0]
		
        my_server.clientManager.forwardCommand(ClientManager.ClientManager, message=receivedData)
        my_server.clientManager.dispatcher.dispatchCommand(receivedData)
        await sio.emit('DeviceState', "state")
```

Okay, great. Based on this code, in the Socket.IO server you can access the dispatcher as `self.clientManager.dispatcher`, so the following should work:

```python
class Server_SocketIO:
        # ...

	@sio.on('SetDeviceParameters')
	async def on_SetDeviceParameters(sid, *args):
		receivedData = args[0]
		
		self.clientManager.forwardCommand(ClientManager.ClientManager, message=receivedData)
		self.clientManager.dispatcher.dispatchCommand(receivedData)
		await sio.emit('DeviceState', "state")
```

@piiswrong The problem is that we're passing a default compute capability of 30 which works on p2 instances, but will fail for most generations of GPUs.  I'm looking into adding a switch to detect this and use the correct values.  

Have verified that:
```python
module = mx.rtc.CudaModule(source, options='--gpu-architecture=compute_30')
```
and
```python
module = mx.rtc.CudaModule(source, options='--gpu-architecture=compute_37')
```
Work on P2 instances (K80), and additionally that
```python
module = mx.rtc.CudaModule(source, options='--gpu-architecture=compute_62')
```
Works on a Jetson TX2.

@piiswrong I think this change introduces a regression for symbols w/ SoftmaxOutput losses and perplexity metrics:
Before, one could apply SoftmaxOutput to 'flattened data': (batch_size * seq_len, classes) and compute mx.metric.Perplexity on these flattened predictions with a flattened one-hot label array (the metric also flattened the label shape).
After this change ( and #8003 ), the update_metric call requires the first axis to be the batch_size only (no collapsing between batch and seq_len allowed anymore). If this is changed accordingly in the symbol (namely using SoftmaxOutput with preserve_shape=True), returning (batch_size, seq_len, vocab_size) predictions, and using non-flattened one-hot labels, the Perplexity metric fails in update(), as labels are flattened internally, but not pred (in line metric.py::652).
So it seems that either all metrics should support multi-dimensional input, or the slicing from #8003 should be made less strict.

This was documented in the upgrade guide.
https://jquery.com/upgrade-guide/3.0/#breaking-change-and-feature-jquery-deferred-is-now-promises-a-compatible

I agree it's probably impossible to warn about this in Migrate, we considered ways to do it without throwing a bunch of spurious and incorrect warnings. In a quick search of code it seemed like most uses were not affected by the change, and of course this is a desired change by people who want consistent Promise/A+ behavior in older browsers like IE11. Those who don't can change `.then()` to `.done().fail()` and get the old behavior, even in 3.x.

The main goal of Migrate is to detect problems and give enough information to allow a developer to fix them in their code. It's not meant as a a long-term crutch so that something written for jQuery 1.3.2 can continue to run on jQuery 3.2.1 without changes. That is exactly why Migrate 3.x doesn't try to fill everything that was removed, you should [deal with that](https://blog.jquery.com/2016/05/19/jquery-migrate-1-4-1-released-and-the-path-to-jquery-3-0/) before trying to upgrade to jQuery 3.x. If you "can't" do that then stay with jQuery 2.x until dependencies are compatible with 3.x.

There are currently no configuration flags in Migrate. In a mixed-code environment where plugins from different sources expect different jQuery versions, there is no flag that can provide the legacy behavior that each plugin expects. If you need behavior from a specific older version of jQuery then by all means use that version and don't upgrade jQuery. If you have control over the code, either because it's yours or you can submit a PR, then upgrade it to work with the later version of jQuery.

@davidjameshowell I think we should rely on the latest version of it https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-pantos-http-live-streaming-20#section-4.3.2.4

```
4.3.2.4.  EXT-X-KEY

   Media Segments MAY be encrypted.  The EXT-X-KEY tag specifies how to
   decrypt them.  It applies to every Media Segment that appears between
   it and the next EXT-X-KEY tag in the Playlist file with the same
   KEYFORMAT attribute (or the end of the Playlist file).  Two or more
   EXT-X-KEY tags with different KEYFORMAT attributes MAY apply to the
   same Media Segment if they ultimately produce the same decryption
   key.  The format is:

   #EXT-X-KEY:<attribute-list>

   The following attributes are defined:

      METHOD

      The value is an enumerated-string that specifies the encryption
      method.  This attribute is REQUIRED.

      The methods defined are: NONE, AES-128, and SAMPLE-AES.

      An encryption method of NONE means that Media Segments are not
      encrypted.  If the encryption method is NONE, other attributes
      MUST NOT be present.

      An encryption method of AES-128 signals that Media Segments are
      completely encrypted using the Advanced Encryption Standard
      [AES_128] with a 128-bit key, Cipher Block Chaining, and PKCS7
      padding [RFC5652].  CBC is restarted on each segment boundary,
      using either the IV attribute value or the Media Sequence Number
      as the IV; see Section 5.2.

      An encryption method of SAMPLE-AES means that the Media Segments
      contain media samples, such as audio or video, that are encrypted
      using the Advanced Encryption Standard [AES_128].  How these media
      streams are encrypted and encapsulated in a segment depends on the
      media encoding and the media format of the segment.  fMP4 Media
      Segments are encrypted using the 'cbcs' scheme of Common
      Encryption [COMMON_ENC].  Encryption of other Media Segment
      formats containing H.264 [H_264], AAC [ISO_14496], AC-3 [AC_3],
      and Enhanced AC-3 [AC_3] media streams is described in the HLS
      Sample Encryption [SampleEnc] specification.  The IV attribute MAY
      be present; 

      The value is a quoted-string containing a URI that specifies how
      to obtain the key.  This attribute is REQUIRED unless the METHOD
      is NONE.

      IV

      The value is a hexadecimal-sequence that specifies a 128-bit
      unsigned integer Initialization Vector to be used with the key.
      Use of the IV attribute REQUIRES a compatibility version number of
      2 or greater.  See Section 5.2 for when the IV attribute is used.

      KEYFORMAT

      The value is a quoted-string that specifies how the key is
      represented in the resource identified by the URI; see Section 5
      for more detail.  This attribute is OPTIONAL; its absence
      indicates an implicit value of "identity".  Use of the KEYFORMAT
      attribute REQUIRES a compatibility version number of 5 or greater.

      KEYFORMATVERSIONS

      The value is a quoted-string containing one or more positive
      integers separated by the "/" character (for example, "1", "1/2",
      or "1/2/5").  If more than one version of a particular KEYFORMAT
      is defined, this attribute can be used to indicate which
      version(s) this instance complies with.  This attribute is
      OPTIONAL; if it is not present, its value is considered to be "1".
      Use of the KEYFORMATVERSIONS attribute REQUIRES a compatibility
      version number of 5 or greater.

   If the Media Playlist file does not contain an EXT-X-KEY tag then
   Media Segments are not encrypted.

   See Section 5 for the format of the key file, and Section 5.2,
   Section 6.2.3 and Section 6.3.6 for additional information on Media
   Segment encryption.
```

This abstraction will only loop on the `batch_write` calls when it's being used as a context manager that has ended and needs to flush any remaining values (likely a small number depending on the configured `flush_amount`). Otherwise, the `batch_write` call will only occur as often as the user calls `put_item` or `delete_item`. The delay between `batch_write` calls will be entirely dependent on how fast the user is producing items.

I'm sure I'm not giving this justice, my eyes are glazing over at the size of this post. So, I'll at least give you some troubleshooting advice first before we dig too deep.

* According to [official docs](http://pubs.vmware.com/vsphere-55/index.jsp#com.vmware.wssdk.apiref.doc/vim.SearchIndex.html#findByUuid) those two flags on the end of `search_index.FindByUuid(None, vm_uuid, True, True)` that you set to `True` are `vmSearch` and `instanceUuid` which means you're looking by the vCenter Instance UUID. The value `vm_uuid` is `row[2]` which may or may not contain the instanceUUID assuming the query you built is correct and *I'm not sure* if that's how you're *intended* to get the vm_uuid. You may be looking at the BIOS UUID which is the UUID the guest knows about itself and not the vCenter UUID.

* You're break point shows a `vcenter_uuid` that does not equal the `vm_uuid` yet your search `FindByUuid(None, vm_uuid, True, True)` is looking for the vcenter ID not the BIOS ID which I presume is what you're locally calling the `vm_uuid`. 

Based on your break point and my analysis of the call you made this is *actually* correct behavior. If you wanted to search and find `vm_uuid` you should *probably* call `FindByUuid(None, vm_uuid, True,` **False**`)` instead!

Feel free to re-open if this doesn't resolve your issue.

```
        gui.label(
            widget=info_box, master=self,
            label="Root mean square error: %(rmse).6s")

It will bind the label to `self.rmse`. In method `apply` you will then have only

        self.rmse = RMSE(predicted)[0]

It's good to be wary when editing core functionality like this, and I agree there should be a good consideration as to what possible use cases and scenarios are involved.

In my (limited) experience I have encountered exclusively situations where it is desirable for the `Wait`  keywords to catch the `StaleElementReferenceException` and fetch a fresh element from the DOM. Of course, I could be wrong, but I can't really imagine an counterexample either. Does someone else maybe know a reasonable use case where the current behavior is desired?

@aaltat I agree with your statement in general, but -and correct me if I'm wrong- I feel that in the case of elements going stale during `Wait` keywords there's not really a decent solution to handle it. If during a wait an element is changed (to some state other than the expected end state), it raises the error and as far as I know there's nothing the enduser can do. I could use `Execute JavaScript return document.GetElementById("element-id").innerHTML == "expected value"` (for example), skipping the staleness problem entirely, but this is not very user-friendly and elegant.

All in all, I guess I agree with @pekkaklarck that it seems (like him and possibly even more so, I'd like to be careful) the `Wait` keywords are a notable exception to the general caution of not editing at the core.

I did some testing and setting the timeout is problematic with Chrome. It is problematic because [Chrome Webdriver](http://selenium-python.readthedocs.org/api.html#module-selenium.webdriver.chrome.webdriver) is based on the [Remote Webdriver](http://selenium-python.readthedocs.org/api.html#module-selenium.webdriver.remote.webdriver) and you should be able to call driver object [set_page_load_timeout](http://selenium-python.readthedocs.org/api.html#selenium.webdriver.remote.webdriver.WebDriver.set_page_load_timeout). In Python, you should do this:
```
from selenium import webdriver

driver = webdriver.Chrome()
driver.set_page_load_timeout(180)
```
But because S2L does not expose the driver object for you, you can not do it. Although it should be easy to create extension keyword to S2L, which does the above for you. 

But in [Firefox Webdriver](http://selenium-python.readthedocs.org/api.html#module-selenium.webdriver.firefox.webdriver), the timeout can be given as (keyword) argument. I did not have page that would take that long to load, but I do not see why this should not work you:
```
    Selenium2Library.Create Webdriver
    ...    Firefox
    ...    timeout\=1
    Selenium2Library.Go To    http://selenium-python.readthedocs.org/api.html#module-selenium.webdriver.firefox.webdriver
    Selenium2Library.Wait Until Page Contains    Selenium
    [Teardown]    Selenium2Library.Close All Browsers
```
If it does not work you, could you run only the relevant keywords and send the log, with ```--loglevel trace``` enabled?
Would the `Wait Until Page Contains Element` keyword solve your problem? If not could you describe your problem better, because I don't understand what problem you are trying to solve?

I'm having the same issue.

I solved my issue by reinstall my openssl following this [link](https://medium.com/@timmykko/using-openssl-library-with-macos-sierra-7807cfd47892)

```
$ brew reinstall openssl
$ brew link --force openssl
$ ln -s /usr/local/opt/openssl/include/openssl /usr/local/include
```

I have modified the PATH variable,before "pipenv install".

Thanks，uranusjr，your command solved my problem.

main = what I need for my app to run in production

dev = all other tools. For yapft, pylint, flake8, sphinx, pytest, etc, use dev-packages. Works great.

I do not need to have pylint, autopep8, pytest in production. pipenv is meant to handle these 2 environments pretty well. All other combinatories are out of the scope of pipenv and frankly, most of the time can be merged into the "other"/dev-packages.

So start with master, which passes all the tests on PyPy. Then make your changes, and (unless your code causes a problem for PyPy) all the tests should still pass. If your code breaks on PyPy, the fixes should be in this PR, not in a separate one.

The "fixes" you seem to be making to PyPy appear to be a set of hacks to PyPy to make our tests work. That's not what we should be doing, rather we should be ensuring our tests continue to work with the versions of PyPy that people are using, so that virtualenv works for them.

If you're arguing that virtualenv should drop support for PyPy, then that's a much bigger proposal, and you'd have to come up with a pretty strong argument for doing so.

Better is for Tox to be set up to do that for you…  My point is that when you ask Tox to run an environment, it should give you accurate coverage results for that run.  The current setup has all of the environments in one pool and makes it hard to clean up.

I think it's reasonable to add a `SlidingWindow<T>`. The `boost::circular_buffer` is cool, but it's more complicated than we need. For example, it has to handle situations like

~~~
[d] [e] [f] [ ] [ ] [ ] [a] [b] [c]
~~~

but a more limited circular buffer would never have to handle that situation. So I think it would be worth having a `SlidingWindow` class which implements a custom circular buffer internally. It won't support element removal -- the only way to remove an element would be by overwriting it by pushing lots of elements. That way the buffer will always be contiguous.

Then this SlidingWindow could have two important methods:

- `getData()` which returns a const reference to internal buffer (maybe a `const vector<T>&`). The buffer might be out of order, but every element will be valid.
- `linearize()` which reorders the internal buffer so that the oldest element is at the beginning. This is optional -- callers could choose to never call this.

Eventually we could add iterator support which iterates in order, but that might not be necessary at the moment.

Internally this class could use a `std::vector`. First we'd use `push_back` until the vector reaches the max size, then subsequent appends would overwrite existing vector elements, and the vector's size would never change again.

Thoughts, @breznak? This would be a little different from this current PR. This PR just uses a vector, so after the window is full, every append causes the whole vector to be copied. My suggestion would involve never removing an element from the vector, and instead keeping a `next_` iterator / counter.

When a  function needs forward_returns or quantiles it will check their existence in factor's columns and if not present they will be calculated and added (to factor). forward_returns and quantiles columns would act as a cache and multiple calls to different tear sheet functions would make use of the cached values without additional computation. This also won't enforce any particular function call order and the details of this implementation are hidden from the user.

hmm, that code is pretty old and it's in a private method, which you're using in a way that actually asks it to "throw away the datatype". My guess is that the intention of that old snippet is to make round-tripping safer (so parsing the n3 of a decimal will at least not lead to an int). Also as `Decimal('10') == Decimal('10.0')`, it seems perfectly safe to me.

So my call is that this would be a rather cosmetic change which is slightly more dangerous in the general case (you could suddenly end up with integer division for example), which is why i'll close this for now. 

Also if you used this (private!) method for formatting, then it's probably a better idea to do this explicitly (and as you like) in your code. If you were simply searching for a datatype preserving method, you can just do `Literal(10, datatype=XSD.decimal).n3()`.

On Apr 27, 2015, at 6:54 PM, Josh Meyers <notifications@github.com> wrote:

> 	• I computed (and then cached) the centroid by computing the DFT. If there's an easier way, I'd love to hear it!
> 
You can calculate the centroid as a sum over your Fourier components using this (which needs some 2\pi factors to be correct…):


The \ast means convolution here.  FT(f) is what you already have and is evaluated on a grid.  The subtlety is that the Fourier transform of the function x is not convergent.  But this actually makes sense because your function is formally periodic once you’ve put it on a grid in k space.  So what you really want (and are doing with the DFT) is taking the centroid of f(x) times a boxcar function spanning the range +-L/2 where L is the total length of your transform.

Hence what you should plug into FT(x) is the Fourier transform of the function
g(x) = x (|x|<L/2), 0 otherwise.  This transform will be purely imaginary.

Please note that you are using both the SVG Framework (all.js) and the webfont version (all.css) of Font Awesome: they are different projects, and you must pick up just one of them.

If you need to use ::before pseudoelement, you should go for the webfont (as per documentation).

Let's say you want to go for the SVG Framework, you should

1. Remove the loading of the CSS
2. Enable the `searchPseudoElements` option
3. Add `display: none` option to pseudoelement
4. Move all the other css rules to a wrapper element

or

1. Do not use pseudoelements: replace `icon-con` with `fas fa-user`

Ref: https://fontawesome.com/how-to-use/svg-with-js#pseudo-elements

Attention, quand tu utilises #+numéro dans un message de commit (comme dans "Add indemnites forfaitaires (#2)", par exemple), la convention est que #+numéro fasse référence à une “issue” (un bug ou une proposition de fonctionnalité) connue.

Par exemple, la “pull request” que tu as ouverte ici est référencée comme sous #164. Ce système de référence te permet d'écrire dans un message de commit, par exemple : `Add indemnités forfaitaires as requested in #155`, ce qui génèrera automatiquement un lien dans l'issue #155, et permettra à une personne intéressée à suivre l'avancement de l'implémentation de toutes les indemnités de sécurité sociale de découvrir cette pull request  :)

Si cela t'intéresse, tu trouveras plus d'informations sur [l'aide de GitHub](https://help.github.com/articles/about-issues/).

Essential commands:
```
git checkout develop # This it to get our correct base branch
git pull --rebase # This is do update
git checkout -b refactored-branch # Creates branch from develop
git cherry-pick <COMMIT IDS SEPARATED BY SPACES> # To find these commits just look at which ones are your changes
git branch -D <old branch>
git branch -m <old branch name> # This renames your current branch
git push
```
Those commands assume you're working from the pycontribs/jira as your "origin" remote. If not, then to get up to date you would need to do something like the following.
```
git remote add upstream git://github.com/pycontribs/jira.git
git fetch upstream
git checkout develop
git rebase upstream develop
```

Thanks for your answer @haotianw465, some comments about your comments

> I think it really depends on who owns those services. If you own those services that serving resource/* and they are also integrated with X-Ray, they will also actively emit segments. The X-Ray service graph will respect the segment emitted from the server side other than caller. So if all resource/1, resource2 are served with a Django app which emits segment named my_service, then in service graph you will only see a node called my_service

Well in an organization that is starting to adopt the tracing technology, where only a few of services have this technology in place it's the perfect scenario which can't trust on the caller to name the node with the proper service name. True that at least for all of the services that you own at some point once the tracing technology is adopted all of the nodes will be named with the proper name. But it will take some time.

In any case, there is the case when you are calling an external service that you don't have any kind of control. In that case, the name of the node that will persist will be always the URL with all of the issues that I've commented.

I'm still thinking that the first part of the URL is the least bad.

Going back to your example of having two different URLs that are prefixed by the same hostname but can end up in a different service, `data/1` and `data/2`. This is a scenario is quite similar to one of he use cases in our infrastructure. Where, there is an HTTP middleware that implements some commonalities such as Auth, Rate limiting and routes the traffic to downstream services using part of the URI to identify it univocally. In that case, I'm still thinking that the best option is to use the hostname to identify the node. So, having a unique node in the AWS Xray console that identifies all of the calls to this intermediate layer, while later, the downstream services that are behind to each URL path will have the chance to be identified also in the AWS Xray console with their proper name.

Thanks so much for support, but even in the postscript hintset case this is semantically incomplete: the given pointTag labels depend on contour order and their respective starting points.

When anything changes, the label will survive but the hint replacement will have to be regenerated, most likely resulting in different hint sets and pointTag labels to become valid again. And the information needed to do so isn’t encoded in the autohinter output. ‘Links’ would be more useful than raw hint positions and widths.

For higher level hinting commands as in VTTtalk we would like to see point tag references to source point indices and to target points that become touched (in horizontal or vertical direction), and the ascii strings that make up the actual command then need to be marked to make clear which arguments are indices… possible but not simple. And perhaps better suited to a less generic representation…

Hi @nuschk! PSQ is not an official Google product, it's intended as an example of how to achieve one use case using Pub/Sub. If you decide to use it in production support falls on you. Of course, if there are issues with Pub/Sub itself then that is a different story.

That said, it's a relatively small library (one developer could conceivably understand all of it in one or two days) and can be used as a good starting point for creating your own library. If you're interested in a out-of-the-box production-ready task queue service, you might want to sign up for our [Cloud Tasks alpha](https://goo.gl/Ya0AZd).

> What's the best way to use it in CI for testing?

You can use the emulator and run a worker if you want. You can also just manually call [`queue.deque`](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/psq/blob/master/psq/queue.py#L98) yourself to inspect the tasks that were queue and even [execute them](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/psq/blob/master/psq/worker.py#L67) if you want. In theory what you are saying is correct; in practice I have 10s of jobs/job classes. For all of them I have to override getJobDisplayName that requires me to add a member variable called name to each, then add a setter and/or a constructor to set that name for the job on creation.

Now that sounds tedious, right... I should have a parent class that I inherit from which has that behaviour, except that I am mandated by the framework to inherit from Job. The alternative is for me to Have a set of superclasses called something like NamedJob or something that extends each of the 6 types of Job<>.

As for the immutability of the Job, again that should be true, however, every change of status that requires the persistence of a job instance re-saves the display name. So even though I have no intention of changing the display names of jobs, the design does not reflect that requirement at least not when it comes to the display name.

Implementation plan:

- [ ] Add a `Bucket.requester_pays` boolean property, mapped onto the `billing/requesterPays` subfield defined in the [REST discovery document](https://www.googleapis.com/discovery/v1/apis/storage/v1/rest).
- [ ] Add an optional `user_project` constructor parameter to `Bucket.__init__`:  we cannot know whether it is valid / appropriate to be set during the constructor, so we just trust the user.
- [ ] If the bucket's `user_project` is set, all API requests made by its methods will pass the value as the `userProject` query parameter. 
- [ ] When constructing a `Blob`, copy its bucket's `user_project`:  likewise, send the `userProject` query parameter to all API requests if it is set. 
- For both `Bucket` and `Blob` API requests, we rely on the back-end to raise errors for cases where `userProject` should not be passed (i.e., the bucket is not configured for `requesterPays`), and for cases where it is required but not passed.

# repro_4293.py
from __future__ import print_function

import multiprocessing
import os
import sys

from google.cloud import storage


CONTENT = b'Zooooooooooooooooooooooom.'
BUCKET = u'' SYNTAX-ERROR-EEEK!
BLOB_NAME = u'foo.txt'


def get_local_blob():
    client = storage.Client()
    bucket = client.bucket(BUCKET)
    return bucket.blob(BLOB_NAME)


def populate_blob():
    blob = get_local_blob()
    if blob.exists():
        print('Blob already exists.', file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)
    blob.upload_from_string(CONTENT)


def get_contents(filename):
    current_process = multiprocessing.current_process()
    print('current_process: {}'.format(current_process))
    blob = get_local_blob()
    blob.download_to_filename(filename)


def get_filename(base_name):
    curr_dir = os.path.dirname(__file__)
    result = os.path.join(curr_dir, base_name)
    return os.path.abspath(result)


def verify_contents(filename):
    with open(filename, 'rb') as file_obj:
        contents = file_obj.read()

    name = os.path.basename(filename)
    same = contents == CONTENT
    msg = '{} as expected? {}'.format(name, same)
    print(msg)


def clean_up(*filenames):
    for filename in filenames:
        os.remove(filename)

    blob = get_local_blob()
    blob.delete()


def main():
    # 1. What version?
    print('storage.__version__: {}'.format(storage.__version__))

    # 2. Populate the blob in the bucket.
    populate_blob()

    # 3. Get two filenames (for current process and subprocess).
    filename1 = get_filename('here1.txt')
    filename2 = get_filename('here2.txt')
    if os.path.exists(filename1) or os.path.exists(filename2):
        print('File exists.', file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)

    # 4. Download to a file in current process.
    get_contents(filename1)

    # 5. Download to a file in a subprocess.
    process = multiprocessing.Process(
        target=get_contents, args=(filename2,))
    process.start()
    process.join()

    # 6. Make sure we downloaded what we expected.
    verify_contents(filename1)
    verify_contents(filename2)

    # 7. Clean up the created blob and files.
    clean_up(filename1, filename2)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

@Temikus We don't attempt to restrict applications "scribbling" other attributes onto our client-side objects:  Python's development culture follows a "consenting adults" model, rather than the "bondage-and-discipline" model of Java and other static typing languages.

The properties we *do* define map "Pythonic" `attribute_names_with_underscores` to the "Javiotic" `namesWithCamelCase` versions required by the back end:  in some cases, we also work around other issues (e.g., the API defines names which are Python keywords / builtins).  Using `__slots__` is normally a performance / memory optimization, rather than a "type safety" option.

@xapple: `pp_map` is deprecated, so I didn't notice that it hadn't moved along with recent updates.  Thanks for catching that.  I've made a patch to correct it, f46fa7a12964a7d517ef71ae14cc05e73df8a8b1.

The following test code should work for you, with the patch:
```
import pathos
print pathos.__version__
from pathos.pp_map import pp_map
f = lambda i: i*2
print pp_map(f, [1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 8])
```

However, please note that the serialization of lambdas is a bit tricky, and even more so  when serializing by saving the source code (as is done by `ppft`, which is used by `pathos`).  The lambdas serialize by looking up the name reference (in this case, `f`) in the buffer memory... and thus, it can sometimes get it wrong if there are multiple reuses of `f` as a name reference in the recent buffer memory.  Note that the lambda is explicitly given a name reference `f` above, instead of declaring it inside the `pp_map` call.  This tends to yield better serialization results.

Note again that `pp_map` is a deprecated interface -- the preferred interface is identical to the `Pool` interface for `multiprocess`, as shown here:

```
>>> import pathos
>>> p = pathos.pools.ParallelPool()
>>> f = lambda i:i**2
>>> p.map(f, range(4))
[0, 1, 4, 9]
>>> p.close()
>>> p.clear()
>>> 
```

If you'd like to fold in some of the features of your own `prll_map`, please feel free to submit a PR.

Although bold and the character style Strong appear the same when rendered, they use two different mechanisms. The first applies bold directly and the second applies a character style that can include any number of font characteristics.

To identify all occurrences of text that appears in bold, you may need to do both.

But to just find the text having bold applied you would do something like this:

```python
for paragraph in document.paragraphs:
    for run in paragraph.runs:
        if run.bold:
            print run.text
```

Note there are ways this can miss text that appears bold, like text that appears in a paragraph whose font formatting is bold for the entire paragraph (Heading1 for example). But I think this is the property you were looking for.

First a couple of clarifying questions:

1. What exactly do you mean by re-direction to the browser?
0. What is the purpose of the first 3 lines? I notice that you are building a service object with `v3`, note that PyDrive is currently using `v2`, which has differently named fields. As long as you are doing fairly standard operations on your files, you will not need to build your own service object as PyDrive takes care of this under the hood.
0. Are you calling this code in some sort of loop? In that case I would recommend creating `gauth` and `drive` only once and to refer to these object rather than rebuilding them.

For silent authentication flows, have a look in the [docs for authentication flows](https://googledrive.github.io/PyDrive/docs/_build/html/oauth.html#sample-settings-yaml) if you haven't already. In the example in the docs, you can see the line `save_credentials: True` which ensures that you will only have to go through the authentication process once.

> Also is there any way to know the file's webViewLink [...]?

If you know the ID, you can do it as follows:
```python
file_id = '<your ID>'
file_obj = drive.CreateFile({'id': file_id})
file_obj.FetchMetadata(fields='webViewLink') 
view_link = file_object.metadata.get('webViewLink')
```
You can find the docs on `FetchMetadata()` [here](https://googledrive.github.io/PyDrive/docs/_build/html/filemanagement.html#handling-special-metadata). 
**Note:** you will need the GitHub version of the library for `FetchMetadata()` to accept the `fields` parameter. Installation with pip is trivial and is described [here](https://github.com/googledrive/PyDrive#how-to-install).

Well, it would be ideal if we could fit two 16:9 cards side by side on mobile, but I believe that was discussed early on with David as not possible (too much leftover margin & text gets too truncated). Something I've noticed with these thumbnails is that they provide less meaningful information than the thumbnails. For the future, I am okay with:

Hi @benjaoming! Do you know if we still support Ubuntu Trusty (14.04)? I tested on Ubuntu Xenial (16.04) and Zesty  (17.10), moving `kolibri/dist` to the end of PYTHONPATH will resolve the issue we have with running Kolibri on Python 3. This is because Xenial and Zesty have relatively newer versions of [`python-requests`](https://packages.ubuntu.com/search?keywords=python-requests), which include the `packages` module we need. Ubuntu Trusty's [`python-requests` ](https://packages.ubuntu.com/trusty/all/python-requests/filelist) doesn't include the `packages` module, which makes moving `kolibri/dist` to the end of PYTHONPATH cause another error. I'm wondering that if we don't actively support Ubuntu Trusty now, maybe we can move `kolibri/dist` to the end of PYTHONPATH so it at least works for Ubuntu Xenial and Zesty, as well as OS X?
